summary: The primary muscle involved in pulling the mandible back after a bilateral mandibular fracture in the canine region includes the masseter due to its significant contractile strength despite being underappreciated initially. Other contributing factors include lateral and inferior pulls primarily managed by the anterior belly of the digastric rather than predominantly relying solely on genioglossus or mylohyoid as they are minor forces driving mainly downward/lateral movements instead of substantial rearward traction. Agent1 concluded based on updated research emphasizing the importance of including the masseter among the muscles responsible for pulling the mandible back post-bilateral mandibular fracture at the canine level. Key supporting details highlighted stronger roles played by multiple muscles collectively affecting both vertical alignment correction along with horizontal stabilization necessary when managing such injuries clinically.
